title: Space-Charge Effects in Silicon Reconfigurable Nonlinear-Processing Units
authors: Jonas Kareem, Lorenzo Cassola, Reinier J. C. Cool, Janiek I. van Slooten, Peter A. Bobbert, Wilfred G. van der Wiel
abstract: Reconfigurable nonlinear-processing units (RNPUs) are multi-terminal electronic devices that act as computational primitives, exploiting intrinsic nonlinear charge transport combined with electrostatic tunability. Silicon-based realizations provide a scalable and technologically relevant platform, yet the physical origin of their room-temperature nonlinearity has remained insufficiently understood. Here, we investigate charge transport using temperature- and length-dependent current-voltage measurements on physical devices, complemented by drift-diffusion simulations, and show that transport is governed by space charge. Interface trap states strongly suppress the equilibrium carrier density, while the functional nonlinearity arises from the voltage-dependent competition between injected mobile carriers and fixed ionized background dopants. The resulting non-equilibrium transport exhibits a transition from an Ohmic regime to a strongly nonlinear regime, and ultimately to a velocity-saturation space-charge-limited current regime, as evidenced by the observed voltage and length scaling. We further show that background doping of opposite polarity to the injected carriers controls the onset and strength of the nonlinearity, leading to behavior exceeding the quadratic dependence of the classical Mott-Gurney law. Agreement between experiment and simulation supports that the spatial distribution of injected carriers and fixed charge governs the internal electric-field profile and device response. These results establish a physical framework for silicon-based RNPUs without requiring disorder or hopping transport, and provide design guidelines for reproducible, scalable, and CMOS-compatible implementations of nonlinear computing hardware.
summary: This paper identifies that the nonlinearity in silicon-based computing primitives arises from space-charge-limited conduction rather than disorder-induced hopping. By demonstrating that the effect is driven by the competition between injected carriers and fixed dopants, the authors provide a framework for creating reproducible, CMOS-compatible nonlinear hardware.
main_problem: The physical origin of room-temperature nonlinearity in silicon-based Reconfigurable Nonlinear-Processing Units (RNPUs) was poorly understood, with previous models incorrectly suggesting 2D variable-range hopping.
main_result: The nonlinearity is driven by space-charge effects, specifically the competition between injected carriers and fixed ionized background dopants, which creates a nonlinear electric-field profile.
method: The study combines temperature- and length-dependent current-voltage measurements with drift-diffusion and TCAD simulations to model carrier transport and trap distributions.
model_or_system: The system consists of silicon-based RNPUs with implanted boron or arsenic, featuring a silicon/native oxide interface with high surface trap density and a doping gradient created via reactive-ion etching.
key_observables: Current-voltage (I-V) characteristics, activation energy (Va), power-law exponent (alpha), crossover voltage (Vcross), and effective injected charge (Neff).
important_parameters: Ohmic, quasi-exponential, and space-charge-limited current (SCLC) regimes; interface trap density; background doping polarity; and electrode geometry.
main_assumptions: The model assumes Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics for carrier concentration and assumes that high activation energy is due to interface trap-induced Fermi level pinning.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows I-V curves and logic gate demonstrations; Figure 2 displays Arrhenius plots and band diagrams; Figure 3 presents I-V characteristics for TLM structures and SCLC schematics; Figure 4/5/6/7 show TCAD simulations of voltage scaling, dopant profiles, and device architecture.
paper_structure: The paper begins by characterizing I-V nonlinearity, identifies the role of surface traps in suppressing carriers, proposes a space-charge-limited model, validates it via TCAD simulations, and concludes with design guidelines for scalable hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
